I272780690661.bio: Lydia Nora Brueggemann, later Lydia N. Stuenkel, grew up in a Lutheran minister's family that moved from Osmond to Tilden and then to Fremont, Nebraska. She graduated from Immanuel Hospital School of Nursing and worked in obstetrics at Fremont Memorial Hospital, became the first registered nurse at Hormel in Fremont, and later spent 15 years as a nurse at Arbor Manor. She was deeply involved at Trinity Lutheran Church in Fremont, teaching Sunday School for 20 years, singing in the choir, driving for Meals on Wheels, and serving as a volunteer grandma for the church day care.
funFacts: She was the fourth of seven children of Rev. William and Lydia (Noack) Brueggemann.
Her obituary says she was the first registered nurse at Hormel in Fremont.
She and Edmund H. Stuenkel Jr. married on 17 June 1952 and had three children.
Trinity Lutheran School in Fremont, which her obituary says she attended, traces its beginnings to an 1884 rented building on Main Street.

A Find a Grave memorial notes that the 1920 census listed him, as William, in McCook Ward 1, Red Willow County, Nebraska, with his widowed mother.
In 1934 he conducted Emma Giesselmann’s funeral at Trinity Lutheran Church in Fremont before “a very large attendance,” according to a contemporary Arlington Review obituary transcript.

I272780754245.bio: Caroline Henrietta Harnisch appears in the Lutheran Museum account as Carolina Harnisch, daughter of Adolph and Christine Harnisch. The article says she married Carl Noack in St. Louis in 1882, and that Carl was from Dolton, Illinois and was a Lutheran pastor. Her family belonged to the old German Lutheran network that connected Trinity Lutheran Church in St. Louis, Concordia Seminary students, and later Lutheran pastor families.

The author highlights Harnisch, Pfotenhauer, and Noack descendants as later Lutheran pastors.

I272780754292.bio: Philip Anton Weyel was a Lutheran pastor and early missionary-organizer in northeastern Illinois. Immanuel Evangelical Lutheran Church in Mokena says Anton August Philip Weyel was appointed missionary for Will County, traveled on foot through the Chicago-area settlements, and helped organize German Lutheran families in Frankfort Township in 1850. St. John's Evangelical Lutheran Church, Eagle Lake says Anton Weyel was the first resident pastor of Zion in Crete and was missionary for Will County and the surrounding country, using Crete as his base. Concordia Historical Institute preserves the Philip Anton Weyel Papers, described as correspondence, manuscripts, and diplomas in German.
funFacts: Immanuel's history says Pastor Weyel traveled with a gun and his retriever, Phylax, while visiting scattered Lutheran families.
The Immanuel congregation was organized on 4 November 1850 with fifteen charter members.
Find a Grave places Rev. Phillip A. Weyel's burial at Pilgrims Rest Cemetery in Milwaukee.
Meyers Gazetteer places his birthplace Herborn in Prussian Hessen-Nassau and notes both Catholic and Protestant parish churches there.
St. John’s Eagle Lake history notes that early church-book entries from the Will County congregations were written in German, often using old-style German script.

I272780754264.bio: Johann Adolph Harnisch was part of the Stephanite Lutheran immigration from Saxony to America. The Lutheran Museum article says he was 12 when his family sailed on the Olbers in 1838 and arrived in New Orleans with the Stephanite group in 1839; unlike many of the group, the Harnisch family stayed in St. Louis rather than moving to Perry County. He worked as a shoemaker in 1850 and 1860, later as a private watchman in 1870 and 1880, and died in 1889 while apparently living with the Dolton, Illinois household of his daughter Caroline and pastor son-in-law Carl Noack.
I272780754264.immigration: Sailed from Germany with the Stephanite Gesellschaft aboard the Olbers in 1838 and arrived at New Orleans in 1839; the family remained in St. Louis.
funFacts: The Olbers was also the ship on which Rev. Martin Stephan traveled.
Adolph and Christine Harnisch married at Trinity Lutheran Church in St. Louis on 28 May 1854, by Rev. Friedrich Wyneken.
The Lutheran Museum article says Adolph and Christine were buried at New Saxon Cemetery, later Concordia Cemetery, in St. Louis.
The Lutheran Museum article says the 1850 census repeated the same four-person Harnisch family seen on the Olbers passenger list and marked Adolph as a shoemaker like his father.
By 1870 he had changed trades from shoemaker to private watchman.
records: Lutheran Heritage Center & Museum article on Adolph Harnisch

citations.label: Adolph Harnisch – An Original Stephanite

I272780754268.bio: Christine Johanna Hagemann was born in Hohenassel, Germany and became the wife of Stephanite immigrant Adolph Harnisch. The Lutheran Museum article says Adolph Harnisch married Christine Hegemann at Trinity Lutheran Church in St. Louis on 28 May 1854, with Rev. Friedrich Wyneken officiating. The couple had six baptism records in Trinity's books, and Christine died in 1879 at age 51.
I272780754268.immigration: The Lutheran Museum article reports some evidence that Christine came to the United States in 1847, but the author could not locate her in the 1850 census; treat that arrival year as provisional.
funFacts: Her marriage was performed by Rev. Friedrich Wyneken, later the second president of what became the Lutheran Church–Missouri Synod.
Her daughters Elizabeth and Caroline both married Lutheran pastors.
Six Harnisch children have baptism records in Trinity Lutheran Church, St. Louis, according to the Lutheran Museum article.
The same article says their first child died as an infant before the 1860 census.
records: Lutheran Heritage Center & Museum article on Adolph Harnisch

citations.label: Adolph Harnisch – An Original Stephanite

I272780754522.bio: Gottfried Harnisch was the father of Johann Adolph Harnisch and one of the German Lutheran immigrants associated with the Stephanite Gesellschaft. The Lutheran Museum article says Gottfried and Friedericke Harnisch joined the group led by Rev. Martin Stephan, sailed on the Olbers, and were listed on the New Orleans passenger list with their children Johann Adolph and Anna Dorothea. The passenger-list discussion identifies Gottfried as a shoemaker from Dresden.
I272780754522.immigration: Sailed with the Stephanite immigration aboard the Olbers in 1838 and arrived at New Orleans in 1839; the family stayed in St. Louis.
funFacts: The article says the Harnisch surname is one of the bold-face original immigrant names in the German Family Tree used by the Lutheran Heritage Center & Museum.
In the 1860 census, the Lutheran Museum article says Gottfried’s household was listed directly above his son Adolph’s, with both men called shoemaker masters.

I272780691877.bio: Berend "Ben" Lourens was born in Bennekom, Ede, Gelderland, on 13 January 1911, with his father Albertus recorded as a farmer. His Iowa obituary says he came to America at age two with his family, married Gertrude Van Dusseldorp in Monroe on 25 February 1937, and farmed for years near Prairie City and Runnells before moving into Prairie City in 1984. He died in 2003 at age 92 and was buried after services at First Reformed Church in Prairie City.
funFacts: In retirement Ben enjoyed woodworking and gardening.
His obituary names two surviving sons, five grandchildren, seven great-grandchildren, and a large Lourens sibling group.
Memorials in Ben's obituary were directed to First Reformed Church in Prairie City.
I272780691877.immigration: Ben's obituary reports that he came to America at age two with his family; since his Dutch birth record is dated 13 January 1911, the family immigration was about 1913.
records: 1911 civil birth registration, Bennekom/Ede, Gelderland (Berend Lourens)

I272780692340.bio: Albertus Lourens was recorded as a 26-year-old farmer from Ede when he married Aartje Snetselaar in Ede on 3 March 1900. He was again recorded as a farmer in the 1911 Bennekom birth record of his son Berend (Ben) Lourens. Ben's obituary says Ben came to America at age two with his family, and an obituary for son Albert places the family in rural Prairie City, Iowa, by Albert's birth on 1 December 1913.
funFacts: The obituary of his son Albert names a large sibling group: Garrett, John, Ben, Orie, George, Gertrude, Bertha, Elizabeth, Pete, Geurt, and Jeanett.
Dutch records show the family within Ede municipality before emigration, including Ede, Veenendaal, Bennekom, and Lunteren-area records.
I272780692340.immigration: Ben Lourens's obituary reports that Ben came to America at age two with his family; because Ben was born in January 1911 in Bennekom and Albert was born in rural Prairie City in December 1913, Albertus and Aartje likely immigrated with their children about 1913.

She married farmer Albertus Lourens at Ede on 3 March 1900. Her son Ben's obituary says he came to America at age two with his family, placing Aartje in the Lourens family's move from Gelderland to Iowa about 1913.
funFacts: Aartje's records connect three generations of the line: her parents Geurt and Jannetje, her husband Albertus, and her son Berend/Ben.
An obituary for son Albert shows the family became part of the Prairie City/Pella-area Dutch community in Iowa.

I272780692686.bio: Peter appears in Dutch civil records as Peter Louwrens/Lourens, born at Veenendaal on 14 April 1833 to Johannes Louwrens and Hendrika Jeurs. At his 21 June 1862 marriage to Gerritje Aartsen in Ede, he was age 29 and recorded as a farmer. He was still listed as farmer and father of the groom when his son Albertus married Aartje Snetselaar in 1900.
funFacts: His surname appears with both Louwrens and Lourens spellings in civil records.
Peter's 1833 birth record names his father Johannes as a day laborer, while Peter himself was recorded as a farmer by marriage age.
Peter was still recorded as a landbouwer (farmer) in his 1902 death record at age 68.
His death record gives his birthplace as “Veenendaal Gelders (Ede),” reflecting the local Veenendaal/Ede geography seen across this branch.

I272780753298.bio: Gerritje Aartsen was recorded as a 26-year-old bride born in Stichtsch Veenendaal when she married Peter Lourens in Ede on 21 June 1862. The marriage record names her parents as Aart Aartsen and Jacoba Hartman and records no occupation for Gerritje. In 1900 she appears again in the Ede marriage record of her son Albertus Lourens to Aartje Snetselaar.
funFacts: Her 1862 marriage record ties the Lourens line back to the Aartsen/Hartman family of the Veenendaal area.
She lived to see son Albertus marry into the Snetselaar family, the couple who later anchored the Iowa Lourens line.
Gerritje outlived Peter by seventeen years; her 1919 death record says she died at Ede at age 83.
Her death record again names Aart Aartsen and Jacoba Hartman as her parents and Peter Lourens as her partner.

These records place Johannes in the working-class Veenendaal/Ede community before later Lourens generations became farmers.

The records show the family staying in the Veenendaal/Ede area across at least 1833-1862.

Geurt himself was recorded as a laborer when he married Jannetje Jacobsen in Ede on 25 May 1878. By his death in Ede on 19 December 1923, the civil death record recorded him as a farmer, age 69, born in Ede.
funFacts: His 1923 death record names three (ex-)partners: Maaike van der Wielen, Jannetje Jacobsen, and Hermijntje Schimmel.
He is a clear example of occupational mobility in the line, appearing first as laborer and later as farmer.

I272780753199.bio: Jannetje Jacobsen was recorded as a 20-year-old bride born in Barneveld when she married Geurt Snetselaar in Ede on 25 May 1878. The marriage record names her parents as Berend Jacobsen, a laborer, and Elisabeth Boshuis. She appears again as mother in the 1879 Lunteren birth record of daughter Aartje Snetselaar, who later married Albertus Lourens and immigrated to Iowa.
funFacts: Her daughter Aartje connects the Jacobsen/Boshuis family into the later Lourens immigrant family in Iowa.
Geurt's 1923 death record still names Jannetje among his (ex-)partners.
Jannetje died young: her civil death record says she died in Ede on 21 February 1897 at age 39.
Her death record names Geurt Snetselaar as her partner, helping explain why Geurt's later death record lists multiple partners.

I272780753226.bio: Berend Jacobsen was born at Ede on 17 January 1822 to Jan Jacobsen, a day laborer, and Teunisje Berends. In the 1878 Ede marriage record of daughter Jannetje Jacobsen to Geurt Snetselaar, Berend was recorded as a laborer. His 1893 death record gives him as a 71-year-old day laborer born in Ede who died at Kallenbroek, with Lisebeth Boshuis named as partner.
funFacts: The Jacobsen line shows repeated laboring occupations: Berend's father was a day laborer, and Berend was later recorded as laborer/day laborer himself.
His daughter Jannetje's marriage brought the Jacobsen/Boshuis family into the Snetselaar line.
At his 1848 marriage to Lisebeth Boshuis, Berend was recorded as a 26-year-old boerenknecht, or farmhand.
The same marriage record described his father Jan Jacobsen as a daglooner, reinforcing the family's laboring background.

She is also named as Lisebeth Boshuis in the 1893 death record of Berend Jacobsen, where she is identified as his partner or former partner. These records place her in the Barneveld/Ede network that led into the Snetselaar and Lourens families.

I272780691885.bio: Gertrude Margaret Van Dusseldorp Lourens was born in Prairie City, Iowa, on 22 November 1914 to Gysbert and Hilda Van Dusseldorp. Her obituary says she was educated in rural schools, graduated from Prairie City High School in 1932, married Ben Lourens in Monroe on 25 February 1937, farmed with him in the Prairie City and Runnells areas, and remained a member of First Reformed Church in Prairie City from youth. She died at age 101 at Mill Pond in Ankeny in 2016.
funFacts: Gertrude was educated in rural schools before graduating from Prairie City High School in 1932.
She made profession of faith at First Reformed Church in Prairie City at a young age and was described as a member ever since.
Her obituary named nine great-grandchildren when she died at age 101.

That record places the family around Garderen/Ede through Aart, who was born at Garderen and worked as a farmer. The record does not state an occupation for Grietje, but it clearly links her to Evert Aartsen and their son Aart.

The record names Hendrik Wesloo as father, Anna Kempers as mother, and Hendrik Reurix and Rebecca Huijnen as witnesses. Because the record gives only the child’s first name, the surname connection rests on the named father rather than on a full child surname in the baptism entry.
funFacts: Place context: the baptism was recorded at Amsterdam’s Noorderkerk, one of the city’s Dutch Reformed churches.

I272780759650.bio: Hendrik Wesloo and Anna Kempers registered their intended marriage in Amsterdam on 31 July 1739. He appears as father in the 1741 baptism of son Hendrik at the Noorderkerk and again in the 1745 baptism of Anna Katriena. These church records place the Wesloo/Kempers household in Amsterdam’s Dutch Reformed records in the 1739-1745 period.
funFacts: The surname is indexed as Wesloo in the Amsterdam records, close to the later Wesselo/Wisselo forms used in this branch.
The 1745 baptism record names Barent Geel and Kaatje de Wilde as witnesses, linking the Wesselo and Geel/de Wilde family circles in the same church register.

A 1740 baptism in the Geel/de Wilde family names Johanna Kempe as a witness, likely the same family circle but recorded with a variant form of the name.

In 1737 they baptized Anthonij at the Noorderkerk, with Sijmon Ostman and Sara Groen as witnesses; in 1740 they baptized Hendrik at the Nieuwe Kerk, with Hendrik Wesseloo and Johanna Kempe as witnesses. These records place the Geel/de Wilde household in Amsterdam church life and connect it with the Wesselo/Kempers family circle.
funFacts: The 1737 and 1740 baptisms show the family using both Barent and Barend spelling forms.
The witness list on the 1740 baptism is a useful social-network clue because it names Hendrik Wesseloo and Johanna Kempe.

I272780759680.bio: Catrina de Wilde appears in the Amsterdam baptism records for this family under the common Dutch form Kaatje de Wilde. She and Barent/Barend Geel are named as parents in the 1737 baptism of Anthonij at the Noorderkerk and the 1740 baptism of Hendrik at the Nieuwe Kerk. In 1745, Kaatje de Wilde and Barent Geel served as witnesses for a Wesselo/Kempers baptism, showing the families in the same church network.
funFacts: The records do not spell her as Catrina; they use Kaatje, so the link should be treated as a documented name variant rather than a separate new fact.
Her family’s baptisms span two Amsterdam churches: Noorderkerk and Nieuwe Kerk.

I272780762187.bio: Jan Wisloo appears in Amsterdam marriage records at the start of the 1700s. He registered an intended marriage with Beatrix Claasz Bremmers on 24 September 1701; after her death, his 1703 intended marriage to Anna Lijflant explicitly notes his earlier wife as Beatrix Claasz Bremmers. A 1726 Amsterdam burial for Jan Wisselo likely belongs to the same surname cluster, but the burial index gives no relationships, so it should be treated cautiously.
funFacts: The Amsterdam indexes preserve several spelling variants for the surname, including Wisloo, Wilslo, Visloo, and Wisselo.
The 1701 and 1703 ondertrouw records are especially useful because they name prior spouses, helping separate repeated marriages in a busy Amsterdam population.

She registered an intended marriage with Jan Wisloo on 24 September 1701; the record notes her earlier husband as Hendrik Dircksz Smit. She was buried in Amsterdam on 10 January 1703, with the burial index identifying her as the wife of Jan Visloo.
funFacts: Her 1701 marriage record and 1703 burial record preserve two forms of her name: Beatrix Claasz Bremmers and Beatris Bremmers.
The burial place note names the Heiligewegs- en Leidsche Kerkhof in Amsterdam.

I272780690692.bio: Joyce Kathryn Roorda, later Joyce Kathryn Lourens, was born in Pella and grew up attending Pleasant Grove and Maple Grove country schools before graduating from Prairie City High School in 1957. After high school she worked for the State Health Department, then focused on raising her sons, Douglas and Kevin. In 1978, when Adventure-Life Reformed Church was organized, she became its secretary and served there for 26 years before retirement.
funFacts: She and Larry Lourens were charter members of Adventure-Life Reformed Church.
Her obituary notes long volunteer service at Caring Hands Food Pantry in Altoona.
Her hobbies included reading, scrapbooking, embroidering, camping, and traveling.
Her obituary calls her the only child of Edward and Minnie Roorda, preserving Wilhelmina Hackert Roorda's family nickname.

I272780753828.bio: Epp R. Roorda was born near Pella, Iowa, to John E. and Adriana Van Maaren Roorda and married Cornelia Vander Wilt in 1897. His Pella Chronicle obituary says he and Cornelia had seven children, and that he was survived by a very large family network of grandchildren and great-grandchildren. He spent his last decade at the Pella Rest Home and died at Pella Community Hospital at age 91.
funFacts: He was a member of First Reformed Church in Pella for about 40 years.
His obituary says he served on the church consistory for a number of years.
At his death he was survived by seven children, 19 grandchildren, 3 step-grandchildren, and 44 great-grandchildren.
Oakwood Cemetery recorded his sons by everyday names: Dave, Robert, Ed, and Henry.

I272780753823.bio: Cornelia Vander Wilt Roorda was born north of Pella, daughter of John Vander Wilt and Gora/Govertje Den Burger. She married Epp R. Roorda on 10 February 1897, and their obituary record names seven living children at her death in 1949. She lived at 1116 Marion Street in Pella at the time of her death.
funFacts: Her obituary says she had 19 grandchildren and 4 great-grandchildren by 1949.
She had a twin sister, Sarah, and the obituary names one brother and nine sisters who predeceased her.
She was described as a faithful First Reformed Church member who attended worship until lingering illness prevented it.
Oakwood Cemetery records her as Mrs. Epp Roorda, née Vander Wilt, buried in the same lot 10 as Epp R. Roorda.

I272780755032.bio: Ruurd, also called John, Epkes Roorda was the youngest child of Epke Alberts Roorda and Froukje Wiegers van der Lune. The family history in IAGenWeb's translation of Greetings from America says he came to America with his parents in 1857 and later married Adrianna Jane van Maren in Pella in 1868. He farmed in the Durham neighborhood and also peddled housewares, eggs, and vegetables door to door.
funFacts: He celebrated his 11th birthday aboard ship on the family's 1857 journey to America.
He and Adrianna Jane van Maren had 12 children: 10 sons and 2 daughters.
His siblings' immigration was staggered: three preceded the parents to America, four traveled with the parents, and two stayed in the Netherlands.
His Pella Chronicle obituary, transcribed on Find a Grave, says he farmed south of the Des Moines River before retiring to Pella.
That obituary says he died at home after a three-week illness and was buried at Eureka Cemetery near Durham.
I272780755032.immigration: Immigrated from Dantumawoude, Friesland, to Pella, Iowa, in 1857 with his parents and siblings Hendrik, Gerrit, and Hinke.

I272780755053.bio: Adrianna Jane van Maren married Ruurd (John) Epkes Roorda on 7 October 1868 in Pella. The family history identifies her as the daughter of Maren and Antje van der Linden and says she was born on 23 September 1849, possibly in Friesland. She and John raised a large Roorda family in the Pella area.
funFacts: The family history lists 12 children for Adrianna and John: Henry, John, Epp, Wieger, Luke, Anna, Andrew, Albert, Izaak, Anne, Gerrit, and Janie.
Her husband's work combined farming near Durham with peddling housewares, eggs, and vegetables door to door.

The household included Gerrit van der Wilt, Sophia Spruit, several older children with farmer occupations, and Jan, age 15, also listed as a farmer. This places him among the Dutch immigrant families who followed the early Pella settlement stream.

His daughter Cornelia Vander Wilt Roorda's obituary says Cornelia was born north of Pella and identifies her parents as John Vander Wilt and Gora/Govertje Den Burger.

In Iowa records she is represented in her daughter Cornelia Vander Wilt Roorda's obituary as Gora/Govertje Den Burger, wife of John Vander Wilt.
funFacts: She was still a child when the den Burger family crossed the Atlantic in 1847.

I272780755066.bio: Klaas Hendrik de Fockert belongs to the Herwijnen De Fockert/Hackert line documented in the Pella Community Memory Database's thirty-one-page Dutch register of the family. A page of that register names Klaas Hendrik de Fockert, born at Herwijnen on 20 December 1840, and records his marriage at Herwijnen on 15 March 1867 to Cornelia Hakkert/Rakkert. The same register continues with their children, including Pieter de Fockert, born at Herwijnen on 5 February 1880.
funFacts: The Pella Community Memory Database describes the De Fockert register as covering the family in Herwijnen from 1601 to 1942 and including a family crest.
PCMD metadata for a related Hackert family file notes that the surname Hackert was previously known as DeFockert.

I272780755090.bio: Cornelia Hakkert/Rakkert is named in the Herwijnen De Fockert family register as the wife of Klaas Hendrik de Fockert. The register page says Klaas Hendrik married her at Herwijnen on 15 March 1867 and identifies her as the daughter of Klaas and Maria van Zee, born at Herwijnen on 15 October 1845. Their children are continued in the register, tying the Dutch De Fockert/Hackert family to the later Pella Hackert line.
funFacts: Her son Pieter de Fockert, born 5 February 1880 at Herwijnen, matches the Peter Hackert later found in the Pella branch.
The source preserves the older Dutch family form De Fockert beside the later Hackert spelling.

I272780753770.bio: Peter Hackert matches Pieter de Fockert in the Herwijnen De Fockert register: the page for children of Klaas Hendrik de Fockert and Cornelia Rakkert lists Pieter de Fockert born at Herwijnen on 5 February 1880. In Pella records, his son Henry Hackert's obituary identifies Henry as the son of Peter and Johanna (De Wild) Hackert and says Henry was born in Pella in 1916. This links Peter's Dutch birth family with the Hackert household south of Pella.
funFacts: His son Henry lived most of his life in the same home south of Pella and worked on the farm all his life, according to Henry's obituary.
Henry Hackert later combined farming with 18 years as a rural mail carrier for the United States Postal Service.

Henry's obituary says he was born in Pella on 23 February 1916, son of Peter and Johanna (De Wild) Hackert, and that he lived most of his life in the same home south of Pella. Through daughter Wilhelmina Hackert Roorda, Johanna's line connects directly into the Roorda/Lourens branch.

I272780756185.bio: Epke Alberts Roorda was born at Rinsumageest in Friesland and married Froukje Wiegers van der Lune at Ferwerd in 1822. The family history says he was a painter and glassmaker, later kept a boarding house, and resumed painting after settling in Marion County, Iowa. He and Froukje emigrated from Dantumawoude to Pella in 1857 with four children, while three children had already gone to America and two remained in the Netherlands.
funFacts: Epke painted landscapes on glass in his spare time after arriving in America.
He and Froukje had 11 children, two of whom died young in Dantumawoude.
According to the History of Pella tradition quoted by IAGenWeb, the couple brought plum trees, rhubarb, roses, and other plants to America.
Their son Hendrik served in Company H, 17th Iowa Volunteers, and died during the Civil War at Corinth, Mississippi.
His 1822 civil marriage record calls him a 23-year-old "verver" from Rinsumageest, matching the family tradition that he worked as a painter.
A Ferwerderadeel notarial deed from November 1822 lists him at Damwoude as a debtor for capital of 1,000 guilders.
I272780756185.immigration: Left Dantumawoude for Pella, Iowa, in 1857 with wife Froukje and children Hendrik, Gerrit, Hinke, and Ruurd/John.

I272780756195.bio: Froukje Wiegers van der Lune was born at Hallum, Ferwerderadeel, daughter of Wieger Pieters van der Lune and Antje Gerrits Kooi. She married Epke Alberts Roorda at Ferwerd in 1822 and was matriarch of a large Roorda family that moved from Friesland to Pella, Iowa. The family history emphasizes that she and Epke were garden lovers as well as immigrants, carrying plants and family traditions into the Dutch community at Pella.
funFacts: The couple had 11 children, and two died young before the family emigrated.
Their family reportedly brought plum trees, rhubarb, roses, and other plants to America.
A PCMD photograph identifies Mr. and Mrs. Epke Alberts Roorda and notes their 30 May 1822 marriage.
Her 1822 civil marriage record lists her as a 21-year-old bride born in Hallum.
The same marriage record names her parents as Wieger Pieters van der Lune and Antje Gerrits Kooi, tying the Hallum family into the later Roorda immigrant line.

I272780756344.bio: Wieger Pieters van der Lune appears in the Napoleonic-era Registres civiques for Ferwerderadeel as a Hallum resident born 2 October 1770 and working as a voerman/charretier, a carter or wagoner. His 1846 death record gives his age as 76 and names his parents as Pieter Fransen and Aukje Jans.
funFacts: The 1811-1812 civic register gives his occupation bilingually as "voerman / charretier," a wagoner or carter moving goods by horse and cart.
His civic-register entry places him at Hallum, the village where his daughter Froukje was born.
His death record says he was 76 when he died in Ferwerderadeel in November 1846.

I272780756917.bio: IJzak Rinses Roorda is named in the Rinsumageest and Sijbrandahuis Reformed baptism book as the father of Hinke, baptized 21 April 1754. That church-book entry anchors the patronymic Roorda line in Dantumadeel before modern civil registration.
funFacts: The baptism index uses the spelling "IJzak Rinses Roorda," showing how the name could shift between IJzak, Izaak, and Ijzak in records.
The record places the family in the Hervormde gemeente Rinsumageest en Sijbrandahuis baptism register.

I272780757103.bio: Rinse Johan Roorda is the earliest Roorda ancestor named in this roster. The broader surname has a documented Frisian noble-family context around Roorda State at Genum, but the available source does not prove that this specific Rinse Johan descends from the Genum, Tzummarum, or Cubaard noble branches.
funFacts: Stinsen in Friesland describes Roorda as an important Frisian family with branches at Tzummarum, Cubaard, and Genum, but says it is difficult to show how those branches connect.
The same source says the Genum Roordas appear in records by 1397 as "Rowertha to Ghenim"; treat this as surname context, not proven ancestry for this tree line.
records: Stinsen in Friesland article on Roorda State at Genum

I272780761759.bio: Gerrit van der Wilt appears in the 1847 Pieter Floris passenger-list transcriptions as a 52-year-old farmer traveling with Sophia, née Spruit, and a large household of children. The list places the family among the Dutch emigrants connected with the early Pella, Iowa settlement movement. This is a passenger-list identification rather than a full biography, but it is a strong match for the Gerrit Vander Wilt household in this branch.
funFacts: The detailed WikiTree transcription lists two sons named Jan in the household, ages 24 and 15, while the IAGenWeb transcription distinguishes them as Jan Sr. and Jan Jr.
The same household included several working-age sons listed as farmers, showing that this was a large agricultural family migration.
I272780761759.immigration: Listed on the Pieter Floris, departing the Netherlands in April 1847 and arriving at Baltimore before the family continued toward the Pella, Iowa Dutch settlement.
records: Pieter Floris passenger-list transcriptions

I272780761753.bio: Sophia Spruit is listed in Pieter Floris passenger-list transcriptions as Sophia, née Spruit, age 47, housewife, in the household of Gerrit van der Wilt. The household included multiple children and was part of the Dutch immigrant stream associated with the founding generation of Pella, Iowa. Her record is clearest as passenger-list evidence and family context rather than a detailed personal biography.
funFacts: The passenger transcription preserves her maiden name directly as “Nee Spruit.”
Her household traveled with many Dutch farm families, matching the agricultural character of the early Pella colony.
I272780761753.immigration: Listed in 1847 on the Pieter Floris with Gerrit van der Wilt and their children, bound for the Dutch settlement stream that became Pella, Iowa.

I272780755187.bio: Gerritje van de Streek was born at Oldebroek on 2 February 1858, daughter of Helmig van de Streek and Janna Sneller. Her birth record describes her father as a 35-year-old day laborer and her mother as age 34. She later appears in Pella, Iowa cemetery evidence as Gerritje De Wild, buried at Graceland Cemetery with dates 1858–1944.
funFacts: Her Dutch birth record uses the spelling “van de Streek,” while the Iowa cemetery memorial uses the Americanized married form “De Wild.”
The birth registration was made the day after her birth, on 3 February 1858.
records: Gelders Archief/OpenArchieven civil birth record

I272780755153.bio: Hendrik, memorialized in Iowa as H “Hendrik” De Wild, was born in the Nijkerk area of Gelderland and died on 17 August 1913. His Find a Grave memorial places his burial at Graceland Cemetery in Pella, Marion County, Iowa. The matching cemetery evidence ties the Dutch De Wild line into the Pella Dutch-American community where Gerritje van de Streek De Wild is also buried.
funFacts: His Find a Grave memorial uses only the initial “H” before the quoted given name “Hendrik,” a common pattern for Dutch names in American cemetery indexing.
The memorial gives his birth as 30 November 1856 in Nijkerk Municipality, Gelderland, Netherlands.

I272780758783.bio: Helmig van de Streek is named in his daughter Gerritje’s 1858 Oldebroek birth record as her father. The same civil record gives his age as 35 and his occupation as “daglooner,” a day laborer. This anchors the Van de Streek family in Oldebroek shortly before the later Iowa branch appears in Pella records.
funFacts: The record’s occupational term “daglooner” points to wage labor rather than land ownership or a trade.

A Nationaal Archief emigrant index includes an 1870 Oldebroek entry for a widowed “Helmig-van de Streek” household; because the forename is omitted, it should be treated as migration context, not proof of Helmig himself emigrating.

I272780758779.bio: Janna Sneller is named in the Oldebroek birth record of Gerritje van de Streek on 2 February 1858. The record gives Janna’s age as 34 and lists her as having no stated occupation, while Gerritje’s father Helmig van de Streek was recorded as a day laborer. This provides a civil-registration anchor for the Sneller and Van de Streek families before the later Pella, Iowa connection.
funFacts: The birth record preserves Janna’s surname in the Dutch form Sneller before the family’s later American context.
The registration was made in Oldebroek, a Gelderland municipality that appears repeatedly in the Van de Streek/Sneller records.

I272780759464.bio: Hendrika Stomphorst was born at Nijkerkerveen on 27 May 1827, daughter of Hendrik Stomphorst and Trijntje Willems. Her birth record says her father was a day laborer and gives both parents’ ages. In Iowa, a Find a Grave memorial for Hendrika Jan Stomphorst DeWild records her death on 8 December 1916 and burial at Graceland Cemetery in Pella.
funFacts: Her Dutch birth record was registered at Nijkerk on 28 May 1827, the day after her birth.
The Iowa memorial preserves the middle patronymic-style “Jan” in the name Hendrika Jan Stomphorst DeWild.

I272780761647.bio: Hendrik Stomphorst is identified in the 1827 Nijkerkerveen birth record of his daughter Hendrika. The record gives his age as 35 and occupation as “daghuurder,” a day laborer or hired hand. This civil record connects the Stomphorst family to Nijkerk and provides the parental link into the later De Wild branch.
funFacts: The Dutch occupational word “daghuurder” literally indicates someone hired by the day.
His daughter Hendrika’s birth was registered in Nijkerk even though the event place is Nijkerkerveen.

I272780761639.bio: Trijntje Willems is named as the mother of Hendrika Stomphorst in an 1827 Nijkerkerveen civil birth record. The record gives Trijntje’s age as 37 and lists no occupation for her, while her husband Hendrik Stomphorst was recorded as a day laborer. This is a compact but reliable civil-registration link for the Stomphorst maternal line.
funFacts: Her record shows the pre-surname patronymic form Willems still functioning as a family identifier in early nineteenth-century Gelderland records.
The same record places the family in Nijkerkerveen, a village within the Nijkerk civil-registration area.

I272780759489.bio: Klaas Cornelis Hakkert’s 1875 Herwijnen death record describes him as a 74-year-old farmer, born in Herwen en Aerdt, and names his parents as Cornelis Hakkert and Hester Verploegh. The same record names Maria van Zee as his partner. A daughter’s 1845 birth record also records Klaas as a 45-year-old farmer in Herwijnen, confirming the family setting used by the later Hackert/De Fockert line.
funFacts: His 1875 death record and daughter Cornelia’s 1845 birth record both call him a “landbouwer,” a farmer.
The records preserve the Dutch Hakkert spelling that later appears beside De Fockert/Hackert forms in Pella family-history materials.

I272780759480.bio: Maria van Zee is named as the mother of Kornelia/Cornelia Hakkert in the 1845 Herwijnen birth record for that child. The same birth record lists Klaas Hakkert as the father and describes Maria as having no occupation. Klaas Cornelis Hakkert’s 1875 death record again names Maria van Zee as his partner, tying the Hakkert and Van Zee families together in Herwijnen records.
funFacts: Her daughter’s birth record uses the spelling “Kornelia,” while later family-history entries use Cornelia.
Maria’s name appears in both a birth record and her husband’s death record, giving two independent civil-registration anchors.

I272780759510.bio: The Pella Community Memory Database’s Dutch “Register of the Family De Fockert in Herwijnen” names a section for the children of Leendert de Fockert and Aartje van Maaren. A note on the preceding page says Leendert de Fockert was a “landbouwer en poldermeester,” meaning farmer and polder official. This source ties him to the Herwijnen De Fockert/Hackert family documented in the same register used for the later Pella Hackert line.
funFacts: A poldermeester helped manage local reclaimed-land water works, a very Dutch civic responsibility.
The same PCMD register is a thirty-one-page Dutch family file covering the De Fockert family in Herwijnen from 1601 to 1942 and includes a family crest.

I272780759506.bio: Aartje van Maaren is named in the Pella Community Memory Database’s De Fockert register as the wife of Leendert de Fockert and mother in the section “Kinderen van Leendert de Fockert en Aartje van Maaren.” The register pages list their children in Herwijnen, continuing onto the next page with Hendrikus, Jantje, and Klaas Hendrik de Fockert. Through Klaas Hendrik, this Herwijnen family connects to the later Hackert line in Pella.
funFacts: The register’s child list links Aartje directly to Klaas Hendrik de Fockert, whose descendants used the Hackert/De Fockert surname forms.
The PCMD metadata describes the De Fockert register as a Dutch genealogy with a family crest.

I272780757791.bio: Stinsen in Friesland documents a Sierck Donia generation at Donia State in Easterein/Oosterend and says one Sierck lived on the state in 1540, sold rents from the Donia estate, and was grietman of Hennaarderadeel from 1550 until his death in 1569. This appears to match the Sierck Siercksz Donia in this tree, though the repeated Sierck names in the Harinxma-Donia line require caution. The same site explains that the Donia house was tied to the prominent medieval Donia family and the Schieringer–Vetkoper conflict known as the Donia War.
funFacts: Donia State was important enough that Stinsen in Friesland says the Doniahuis remained the chief house of Easterein after 1500.
The Donia family gave its name to the Donia War, the 1458–1464 conflict between Schieringers and Vetkopers.
Stinsen in Friesland notes that the state was inherited and occupied through several repeated generations named Sierck, so exact identification should be handled carefully.

I272780757510.bio: AlleFriezen indexes Focke Fransen and Dieucke Auts together in a 14 September 1684 membership/confession entry for the Reformed congregation of Burum and Munnekezijl. Later Kollumerland court guardianship records call Focke Fransen deceased and name Dieucke Auts as mother of their orphaned children Frans, Trijntie/Trijn, and Aut Fockes. These records do not use the Hummersma surname directly, so the Hummersma label should be treated as family-tree context while the Focke-Fransen family relationships are documentary.
funFacts: The 1684 church record says Focke and Dieucke made confession of faith on 14 September 1684.
The 1693 guardianship record names curators for the children after Focke’s death, a glimpse of how orphaned children were legally protected.
records: AlleFriezen/OpenArchieven DTB membership record
AlleFriezen/OpenArchieven Kollumerland guardianship records

I272780757503.bio: Dieucke Auts appears with Focke Fransen in a 1684 Burum and Munnekezijl Reformed membership record. Kollumerland court records from 1693 and 1704 identify her as mother of Frans, Trijntie/Trijn, and Aut Fockes after Focke Fransen’s death. The records place the family around Burum, Munnekezijl, and Kollumerland in late seventeenth-century Friesland, but they do not themselves use the later Hummersma surname.
funFacts: The 1693 guardianship record calls the children “weeskind,” or orphan children, and gives their ages as 10, 6, and 4.
By the 1704 guardianship entry the same children were recorded as 21, 18, and 16, showing the family’s legal trail over more than a decade.

I272780757126.bio: Frans Fockes appears in Kollumerland court guardianship records as a child of the deceased Focke Fransen and Dieucke Auts. The 1693 record gives his age as 10, while a 1704 follow-up gives his age as 21, consistent with a birth in the early 1680s. These records do not use the Hummersma surname directly for Frans, so the surname connection should be treated as family-tree context rather than a wording found in the court entries.
funFacts: The guardianship records name his siblings Trijntie/Trijn Fockes and Aut Fockes alongside him.
The age progression from 10 in 1693 to 21 in 1704 is unusually useful for a seventeenth-century Frisian child.

I272780818115.bio: The NLF Online-OFB family report records Carsten Sprengel's wife not by a Sprengel given name, but as "(Mädchen) N.N."; this is a source-backed correction candidate for the GEDCOM placeholder "(Ehefrau) Sprengel." It gives her as Lutheran, born about 1617, died 03 Oct 1685 in Brase, buried in Mandelsloh, and married about 1637 to Carsten Sprengel. Because the report does not identify her birth surname, the source should be treated as evidence for an unnamed wife rather than proof of a Sprengel maiden name.
funFacts: The report explicitly labels her occupation/status as "Meierin, Hausfrau" and records her age at death as 68 years.

I272780818142.bio: The NLF Online-OFB family report for Carsten Sprengel is a strong match by name, approximate birth year, Brase location, spouse, and son Jürgen Sprengel. It records him as Lutheran, born or baptized in 1617, a Meier, and deceased 14 May 1703 in Brase, with burial in Mandelsloh. Note the report's death date differs from the GEDCOM's 14 Jun 1703, so the original Mandelsloh church book should be checked before changing the tree.
funFacts: The report says, citing H. Jendritza, that Carsten died while on the way back from Mandelsloh to Brase.

I272780818218.bio: A probable but not perfect NLF Online-OFB match is Johann Wilhelm Stünkel: the report gives a Suttorf birth in 1651 and the same death date, 29 Feb 1708. This should be handled cautiously because the GEDCOM calls him Johann Stünckel born 1652 and names a different child, while the OFB report gives spouse Catrina Elisabeth Rodewald, marriage 09 Jul 1678, and child Johann Heinrich Stünkel. The report also names Hans Stünkel and Catharina Stünkel as his parents, creating a likely conflict with the GEDCOM mother Liesabeth Rosep.
funFacts: The NLF report's exact 29 Feb 1708 death date is an unusually strong matching point despite the name and family-structure conflicts.
records: Online-OFB NLF family report I83639 for Johann Wilhelm Stünkel; cites NLF-Bibliothek N 0188, pages 247-260, Wöhler.

I272780754477.bio: Sophie Charlotte Frerking appears in her mother Dorothea Sprengel Frerking’s 1850 census discussion as Charlotte Stuenkle, age 27, born in Germany. She was living in District 46, Lafayette County, Missouri with husband Frederic Stuenkle, two Missouri-born children, and her mother Mary/Dorothea Frerking on the Stuenkel farm.
I272780754477.immigration: Her widowed mother Dorothea emigrated to America with her children in 1845 aboard the ship General Washington from Bremen to New Orleans; Sophie Charlotte is documented in Missouri by the 1850 census.

I272780754558.bio: Dorothea Maria Sprengel was baptized in Esperke in April 1792 and married Johann Dietrich Frerking there in 1811. After Johann’s death, she emigrated with her children to America and was living in Lafayette County, Missouri by the 1850 census, in the household of daughter Sophie Charlotte and son-in-law Frederic Stuenkle. She died in 1868 and was buried in Saint Pauls Lutheran Cemetery in Concordia.
I272780754558.immigration: Dorothea emigrated in 1845 aboard the ship General Washington, departing Bremen and arriving at New Orleans on 24 November 1845.

I272780763897.bio: The Mandelsloh online family database is directly relevant to this branch because its parish coverage includes Amedorf and its church books begin in October 1678. Search-indexed material points to Anne Catharine/Catrina Barbara Schünhoff as a likely spelling variant for Cath Barbara Schumhoff, born in Mandelsloh-area records in 1688 and dying in Amedorf in 1746.
funFacts: The Mandelsloh OFB warns that pre-1749 baptisms often omit mothers’ names, an important caution when using these early Amedorf records.

I272780763503.bio: The online summaries for Anna Maria Breckheimer and Johann Kaspar Müller list a son Johann Peter/Petrus Müller baptized or born on 19 April 1700 in Flörsheim and dying on 28 October 1763. Because German and Latin church records often interchange Peter/Petrus, this is a credible match to the GEDCOM’s Johannes Petrus Müller, but the exact parish entry should still be checked.

I272780764019.bio: A Geneal-Tree search result for Johann Heinrich Müller gives the same Flörsheim dates found in this GEDCOM person—6 July 1636 and 9 November 1694—but the given name differs from Johann Nicol. Treat this as a useful same-place/same-date Müller lead, not as proof that the records are for the same man, until the Flörsheim parish entry is reviewed.
funFacts: This is a good example of why early German research needs spelling and given-name reconciliation rather than automatic merging.
